([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"): *Sistem Lebuh Raya Ekspres Malaysia*; [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 马来西亚高速公路系统) is a network of national [controlled-access expressways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") in [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") that forms the primary backbone network of Malaysian national highways. The network began with opening of the [Tanjung Malim–Slim River tolled road](/wiki/Tanjung_Malim%E2%80%93Slim_River_Highway "Tanjung Malim–Slim River Highway") (part of [Federal Route 1](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_1 "Malaysia Federal Route 1")) which was opened to traffic on 16 March 1966,[[2]](#cite_note-toll-2) followed by the construction of the [North–South Expressway](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_(Malaysia) "North–South Expressway (Malaysia)") (NSE). The system continues to be substantially developed. Malaysian [toll road](/wiki/Toll_road "Toll road")-expressways are built by private companies under the supervision of the government highway authority, [Malaysian Highway Authority](/wiki/Malaysian_Highway_Authority "Malaysian Highway Authority") (abbreviated as MHA; also referred to as *Lembaga Lebuhraya Malaysia* (LLM) in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language")).[[1]](#cite_note-llm-1) While toll-free expressways are built by [Malaysian Public Works Department](/wiki/Malaysian_Public_Works_Department "Malaysian Public Works Department") or *Jabatan Kerja Raya Malaysia* (JKR) in Malay.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

– along the [Federal Route 4](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_4 "Malaysia Federal Route 4") and [Butterworth–Kulim Expressway](/wiki/Butterworth%E2%80%93Kulim_Expressway "Butterworth–Kulim Expressway") E15[[10]](#cite_note-asean-highway-10)

– consists of [New Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/New_Klang_Valley_Expressway "New Klang Valley Expressway") E1 (Bukit Lanjan–Jalan Duta), [Duta–Ulu Klang Expressway](/wiki/Duta%E2%80%93Ulu_Klang_Expressway "Duta–Ulu Klang Expressway") E33 (Jalan Duta–Sentul Pasar and Sentul Pasar–Greenwood), [Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 2](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Middle_Ring_Road_2 "Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 2") 28 (Greenwood–Gombak North Interchange), [Kuala Lumpur–Karak Expressway](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur%E2%80%93Karak_Expressway "Kuala Lumpur–Karak Expressway") E8, [East Coast Expressway](/wiki/East_Coast_Expressway "East Coast Expressway") E8 and [Gebeng Bypass](/wiki/Gebeng_Bypass "Gebeng Bypass") 101[[10]](#cite_note-asean-highway-10)

– along the [MEC Highway](/wiki/MEC_Highway "MEC Highway") FT222, [Tun Razak Highway](/wiki/Tun_Razak_Highway "Tun Razak Highway") FT12 and [Federal Route 1](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_1 "Malaysia Federal Route 1") (Segamat–Yong Peng South Interchange)[[10]](#cite_note-asean-highway-10)
– along the [Second Link Expressway](/wiki/Second_Link_Expressway "Second Link Expressway") E3 [[10]](#cite_note-asean-highway-10)

The **Malaysian Expressway System** ([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"): *Sistem Lebuh Raya Ekspres Malaysia*; [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 马来西亚高速公路系统) is a network of national [controlled-access expressways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") in [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") that forms the primary backbone network of Malaysian national highways. The network began with opening of the [Tanjung Malim–Slim River tolled road](/wiki/Tanjung_Malim%E2%80%93Slim_River_Highway "Tanjung Malim–Slim River Highway") (part of [Federal Route 1](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_1 "Malaysia Federal Route 1")) which was opened to traffic on 16 March 1966,[[2]](#cite_note-toll-2) followed by the construction of the [North–South Expressway](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_(Malaysia) "North–South Expressway (Malaysia)") (NSE). The system continues to be substantially developed. Malaysian [toll road](/wiki/Toll_road "Toll road")-expressways are built by private companies under the supervision of the government highway authority, [Malaysian Highway Authority](/wiki/Malaysian_Highway_Authority "Malaysian Highway Authority") (abbreviated as MHA; also referred to as *Lembaga Lebuhraya Malaysia* (LLM) in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language")).[[1]](#cite_note-llm-1) While toll-free expressways are built by [Malaysian Public Works Department](/wiki/Malaysian_Public_Works_Department "Malaysian Public Works Department") or *Jabatan Kerja Raya Malaysia* (JKR) in Malay.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The expressway network of Malaysia is considered as one of the best controlled-access expressway network in Asia and in the world after [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea").[[5]](#cite_note-utusan1-5) With total length over 5,027 kilometres (3,124 mi), of which 2,996 kilometres (1,862 mi) are toll-free [expressways](/wiki/List_of_expressways_and_highways_in_Malaysia#Non_toll_expressways "List of expressways and highways in Malaysia") and 2,031 kilometres (1,262 mi)[[3]](#cite_note-kkr-3)[[6]](#cite_note-psklm-6) are [toll-expressways](/wiki/List_of_expressways_and_highways_in_Malaysia#Toll_expressways "List of expressways and highways in Malaysia"). All Malaysian toll expressways are [controlled-access highway](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") and managed in the [Build-Operate-Transfer](/wiki/Build-Operate-Transfer "Build-Operate-Transfer") (BOT) system.

There are expressways in [West Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia") and [East Malaysia](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia"), however, the former are better-connected. The North–South Expressway passes through all the major cities and conurbations in West Malaysia, such as [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang"), [Ipoh](/wiki/Ipoh "Ipoh"), the [Klang Valley](/wiki/Klang_Valley "Klang Valley") and [Johor Bahru](/wiki/Johor_Bahru "Johor Bahru"). The [Pan Borneo Highway](/wiki/Pan_Borneo_Highway "Pan Borneo Highway") is the longest toll-free expressways in Malaysia, connects the Malaysian states of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") and [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") with [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei").[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Asian Highway Network

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Asian Highway Network")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/AH2_MAL.svg/120px-AH2_MAL.svg.png)](/wiki/File:AH2_MAL.svg)

Asian Highway route shield.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Malaysia_E2_PLUS_Expressway_Jasin_North_Bound_Distance_Sign.png/330px-Malaysia_E2_PLUS_Expressway_Jasin_North_Bound_Distance_Sign.png)](/wiki/File:Malaysia_E2_PLUS_Expressway_Jasin_North_Bound_Distance_Sign.png)

Expressway distance sign with Asian Highway route shield.

A few major expressways in Malaysia are part of the larger [Asian Highway Network](/wiki/Asian_Highway_Network "Asian Highway Network"). The Asian Highway Network is an international project between Asian nations to develop their highway systems, which will form main routes in the network. Seven Asian Highway routes pass through Malaysia:[[9]](#cite_note-AH-Malaysia-9)[[10]](#cite_note-asean-highway-10)

1. **[AH2](/wiki/AH2 "AH2") Asian Highway Route 2** – along the:
   * [North–South Expressway Northern Route](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_Northern_Route "North–South Expressway Northern Route") E1 (Bukit Kayu Hitam-Bukit Lanjan),
   * [New Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/New_Klang_Valley_Expressway "New Klang Valley Expressway") E1 (Bukit Lanjan-Shah Alam),
   * [North South Expressway Southern Route](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_Southern_Route "North–South Expressway Southern Route") E2 (Nilai Utara-Pasir Gudang),[[11]](#cite_note-asian-highway-11)
   * [North-South Expressway Central Link](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_Central_Link "North–South Expressway Central Link") (ELITE) E6 and;
   * [Johor Bahru Eastern Dispersal Link Expressway](/wiki/Johor_Bahru_Eastern_Dispersal_Link_Expressway "Johor Bahru Eastern Dispersal Link Expressway") (JB EDL) E14.
2. **[AH18](/wiki/AH18 "AH18") Asian Highway Route 18** – along the [Federal Route 3](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_3 "Malaysia Federal Route 3")[[11]](#cite_note-asian-highway-11)
3. **[AH140](/wiki/AH140 "AH140") Asian Highway Route 140** – along the [Federal Route 4](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_4 "Malaysia Federal Route 4") and [Butterworth–Kulim Expressway](/wiki/Butterworth%E2%80%93Kulim_Expressway "Butterworth–Kulim Expressway") E15[[10]](#cite_note-asean-highway-10)
4. **[AH141](/wiki/AH141 "AH141") Asian Highway Route 141** – consists of [New Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/New_Klang_Valley_Expressway "New Klang Valley Expressway") E1 (Bukit Lanjan–Jalan Duta), [Duta–Ulu Klang Expressway](/wiki/Duta%E2%80%93Ulu_Klang_Expressway "Duta–Ulu Klang Expressway") E33 (Jalan Duta–Sentul Pasar and Sentul Pasar–Greenwood), [Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 2](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Middle_Ring_Road_2 "Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 2") 28 (Greenwood–Gombak North Interchange), [Kuala Lumpur–Karak Expressway](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur%E2%80%93Karak_Expressway "Kuala Lumpur–Karak Expressway") E8, [East Coast Expressway](/wiki/East_Coast_Expressway "East Coast Expressway") E8 and [Gebeng Bypass](/wiki/Gebeng_Bypass "Gebeng Bypass") 101[[10]](#cite_note-asean-highway-10)
5. **[AH142](/wiki/AH142 "AH142") Asian Highway Route 142** – along the [MEC Highway](/wiki/MEC_Highway "MEC Highway") FT222, [Tun Razak Highway](/wiki/Tun_Razak_Highway "Tun Razak Highway") FT12 and [Federal Route 1](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_1 "Malaysia Federal Route 1") (Segamat–Yong Peng South Interchange)[[10]](#cite_note-asean-highway-10)
6. **[AH143](/wiki/AH143 "AH143") Asian Highway Route 143** – along the [Second Link Expressway](/wiki/Second_Link_Expressway "Second Link Expressway") E3 [[10]](#cite_note-asean-highway-10)
7. **[AH150](/wiki/AH150 "AH150") Asian Highway Route 150** – along the [Pan Borneo Highway](/wiki/Pan_Borneo_Highway "Pan Borneo Highway").[[11]](#cite_note-asian-highway-11)

#### AH2 border crossing dispute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: AH2 border crossing dispute")]

The status of the route alignment of the Asian Highway 2 crossing the [Malaysia–Singapore border](/wiki/Malaysia%E2%80%93Singapore_border "Malaysia–Singapore border") is in dispute. Malaysia had commissioned the [Second Link Expressway](/wiki/Second_Link_Expressway "Second Link Expressway") E3 as part of AH2 to maintain the primary access-controlled highway status of the route.[[9]](#cite_note-AH-Malaysia-9) Meanwhile, [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") had commissioned the [Johor–Singapore Causeway](/wiki/Johor%E2%80%93Singapore_Causeway "Johor–Singapore Causeway") and the [Bukit Timah Expressway](/wiki/Bukit_Timah_Expressway "Bukit Timah Expressway") as part of AH2, as the Johor–Singapore Causeway is the main gateway to Singapore from Malaysia,[[12]](#cite_note-AH-Singapore-12) which could mean that the [Skudai Highway](/wiki/Skudai_Highway "Skudai Highway") would be included in the route alignment instead of the Second Link Expressway.

The completion of [Johor Bahru Eastern Dispersal Link Expressway](/wiki/Johor_Bahru_Eastern_Dispersal_Link_Expressway "Johor Bahru Eastern Dispersal Link Expressway") (EDL) in 2012, the Asian Highway AH2 route were re-routed to Eastern Dispersal Link Expressway (EDL) from Second Link Expressway. Second Link Expressway were gazetted as a part of Asian Highway AH143.

The Second Link Expressway, the [Ayer Rajah Expressway](/wiki/Ayer_Rajah_Expressway "Ayer Rajah Expressway"), [Marina Coastal Expressway](/wiki/Marina_Coastal_Expressway "Marina Coastal Expressway") and [Kallang–Paya Lebar Expressway](/wiki/Kallang%E2%80%93Paya_Lebar_Expressway "Kallang–Paya Lebar Expressway") were later gazetted as Asian Highway AH143.[[10]](#cite_note-asean-highway-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Chronology of the Malaysian Expressway System](/wiki/Chronology_of_the_Malaysian_Expressway_System "Chronology of the Malaysian Expressway System")

### Interstate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Interstate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/NorthSouth-Expressway.jpg/330px-NorthSouth-Expressway.jpg)](/wiki/File:NorthSouth-Expressway.jpg)

South section of [North–South Expressway](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_(Malaysia) "North–South Expressway (Malaysia)") near [Pedas](/wiki/Pedas "Pedas"), [Rembau District](/wiki/Rembau_District "Rembau District"), [Negeri Sembilan](/wiki/Negeri_Sembilan "Negeri Sembilan"), facing towards [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur") (before being upgraded into six-lane [carriageway](/wiki/Carriageway "Carriageway") from 1 July 2005 until 1 July 2007) with [Titiwangsa Mountains](/wiki/Titiwangsa_Mountains "Titiwangsa Mountains") in the top peak

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/KarakTollPlaza.PNG/330px-KarakTollPlaza.PNG)](/wiki/File:KarakTollPlaza.PNG)

The [East Coast Expressway](/wiki/East_Coast_Expressway "East Coast Expressway") (westbound), looking towards [Titiwangsa Range](/wiki/Titiwangsa_Range "Titiwangsa Range"), [Pahang](/wiki/Pahang "Pahang").

Before tolled expressways were introduced in the mid-1970s, most Malaysians travelled around [Peninsula Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsula_Malaysia "Peninsula Malaysia") on [federal roads](/wiki/Malaysian_Federal_Roads_system "Malaysian Federal Roads system").

The major reasons for building new expressways in Malaysia are the increasing number of vehicles along federal routes, the opening of major ports and airports in Malaysia, and the increasing population in major cities and towns of Malaysia.

In 1966, the Highway Planning Unit was established under the Ministry of Works and Communications.

The first tolled highway in Malaysia was the 20-kilometre-length (12 mi) [Tanjung Malim–Slim River tolled road](/wiki/Tanjung_Malim%E2%80%93Slim_River_Highway "Tanjung Malim–Slim River Highway") ([Federal Route 1](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_1 "Malaysia Federal Route 1")) which was opened to traffic on 16 March 1966. It saved journey time by half an hour, and cars were charged 50 sen, buses and lorries RM1 and motorcycles 20 sen. However, in 1994, with the completion of the North–South Expressway, the toll plaza was removed and it became a toll-free highway.

In 1970, the first comprehensive five-year road development programme was formulated by the Highway Planning Unit, which included expanding rural roads and plans to construct three new highways linking the east and west coasts.

On 27 March 1974, the [Kuala Lumpur–Seremban Expressway](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur%E2%80%93Seremban_Expressway "Kuala Lumpur–Seremban Expressway") was constructed. Funded by a loan by the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), the 63.4-kilometre (39.4 mi) expressway was constructed in three phases; the first phase was from [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur") to [Nilai](/wiki/Nilai "Nilai"), while the second phase was from Nilai to [Seremban](/wiki/Seremban "Seremban"). The third phase was the rehabilitation of the old Federal Route 1 from Kuala Lumpur to Seremban as a toll-free alternative to motorists. The completion of the Kuala Lumpur–Seremban Expressway in June 1977 marking the first step towards the construction of the new interstate expressway known as [North–South Expressway](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_(Malaysia) "North–South Expressway (Malaysia)") (NSE).

The [Kuala Lumpur–Karak Highway](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur%E2%80%93Karak_Expressway "Kuala Lumpur–Karak Expressway") ([Federal Route 2](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_2 "Malaysia Federal Route 2")) was built between 1976 and 1979. Meanwhile, the 900-metre-long (3,000 ft) [Genting Sempah Tunnel](/wiki/Genting_Sempah_Tunnel "Genting Sempah Tunnel") was the first highway tunnel in Malaysia, constructed between 1977 and 1979. The tunnel was opened in 1979 by the then Minister of Works and Communications, Dato' Abdul Ghani Gilong.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The first sections of the North–South Expressway were the toll sections of the Kuala Lumpur–Seremban Expressway from Sungai Besi to Labu toll plazas, which was opened on 16 June 1982. The next sections were Bukit Kayu Hitam–Jitra and Senai–Johor Bahru stretches opened in 1985. Then the Ipoh–Changkat Jering and Seremban–Ayer Keroh stretches, which were opened to traffic in 1986. However, on 1 October 1987 the closed-toll system came into force along the Kuala Lumpur–Ayer Keroh and Ipoh–Changkat Jering stretches. The Ayer Keroh–Pagoh stretch on the North–South Expressway was opened to traffic in 1988. All sections of the North–South Expressway were completed and officially opened on 8 September 1994 by the former [Prime Minister of Malaysia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia"), Tun Dr [Mahathir Mohamad](/wiki/Mahathir_Mohamad "Mahathir Mohamad").

Other interstate expressway projects in Malaysia are [North–South Expressway Central Link](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_Central_Link "North–South Expressway Central Link") (opened 1996), [East Coast Expressway](/wiki/East_Coast_Expressway "East Coast Expressway") (opened 2004) and [Kajang–Seremban Highway](/wiki/Kajang%E2%80%93Seremban_Highway "Kajang–Seremban Highway") (opened 2008).

Because of a massive traffic jam on the [North–South Expressway Southern Route](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_Southern_Route "North–South Expressway Southern Route") between [Seremban](/wiki/Seremban "Seremban") and Nilai in [Negeri Sembilan](/wiki/Negeri_Sembilan "Negeri Sembilan"), a new bypass expressway named [Paroi–Senawang–KLIA Expressway](/wiki/Paroi%E2%80%93Senawang%E2%80%93KLIA_Expressway "Paroi–Senawang–KLIA Expressway") was proposed in 2013 to help reduce the traffic jams at the area.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The Phase 2 of the East Coast Expressway ([Terengganu](/wiki/Terengganu "Terengganu")) linking [Jabur](/wiki/Jabur "Jabur") and [Kuala Terengganu](/wiki/Kuala_Terengganu "Kuala Terengganu") was completed on 31 January 2015, thus completing the alignment of the East Coast Expressway from Kuala Lumpur to Kuala Terengganu.

The new project in the west coast of [Peninsula Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsula_Malaysia "Peninsula Malaysia"), [West Coast Expressway](/wiki/West_Coast_Expressway "West Coast Expressway") (WCE) has been unveiled by the government. Construction of the 233-kilometre-long (145 mi) expressway linking [Banting](/wiki/Banting "Banting"), Selangor and [Taiping, Perak](/wiki/Taiping,_Perak "Taiping, Perak") was to start in 2015.

### Greater Kuala Lumpur and Klang Valley

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Greater Kuala Lumpur and Klang Valley")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/SprintHway.JPG/330px-SprintHway.JPG)](/wiki/File:SprintHway.JPG)

The Damansara Link section of Klang Valley's [Sprint Expressway](/wiki/Sprint_Expressway "Sprint Expressway") near Semantan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Lebuhraya_Pantai_Baru.jpg/330px-Lebuhraya_Pantai_Baru.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lebuhraya_Pantai_Baru.jpg)

New Pantai Expressway near [Subang Jaya](/wiki/Subang_Jaya "Subang Jaya").

The history of the highways in the Klang Valley started after [the expulsion of Singapore from Malaysia on 9 August 1965](/wiki/Singapore_in_Malaysia#Expulsion "Singapore in Malaysia"), when the Malaysian government decided to make Port Swettenham (now [Port Klang](/wiki/Port_Klang "Port Klang")) Malaysia's new national port as a replacement for Singapore. As a result, the government planned to build a first highway in Klang Valley known as [Federal Highway](/wiki/Federal_Highway_(Malaysia) "Federal Highway (Malaysia)") connecting Port Swettenham (now [Port Klang](/wiki/Port_Klang "Port Klang")) to Kuala Lumpur in the 1960s.

In 1967, the 45-kilometre (28 mi) Federal Highway (Federal Route 2), the first dual-carriageway highway in Malaysia was opened to traffic.

In the early 1990s the federal government decided to build more expressways and highways in Klang Valley because of the increasing size and population of the Klang Valley conurbation, development of new townships and industrial estates, and the massive traffic jams along Federal Highway.

The [New Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/New_Klang_Valley_Expressway "New Klang Valley Expressway") (NKVE), which was opened in 1990, is the second link to Kuala Lumpur from Klang after Federal Highway. In 1997, [North–South Expressway Central Link](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_Central_Link "North–South Expressway Central Link") (NSECL), which is the main link to [Kuala Lumpur International Airport](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_International_Airport "Kuala Lumpur International Airport") (KLIA) was opened to traffic.

Other expressway projects in Klang Valley are [Shah Alam Expressway](/wiki/Shah_Alam_Expressway "Shah Alam Expressway") (SAE/KESAS) (opened 1997), [Damansara–Puchong Expressway](/wiki/Damansara%E2%80%93Puchong_Expressway "Damansara–Puchong Expressway") (LDP) (opened 1999), [Sprint Expressway](/wiki/Sprint_Expressway "Sprint Expressway") (opened 2001), [New Pantai Expressway](/wiki/New_Pantai_Expressway "New Pantai Expressway") (NPE) (opened 2004), [SMART Tunnel](/wiki/SMART_Tunnel "SMART Tunnel") (opened 2007), KL–KLIA Dedicated Expressway or Kuala Lumpur–Putrajaya Expressway (KLPE) (now [Maju Expressway](/wiki/Maju_Expressway "Maju Expressway") (MEX)) (opened 2007) and [Duta–Ulu Klang Expressway](/wiki/Duta%E2%80%93Ulu_Klang_Expressway "Duta–Ulu Klang Expressway") (DUKE) (opened 2009).

In addition to [Kuala Lumpur Inner Ring Road](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Inner_Ring_Road "Kuala Lumpur Inner Ring Road") (KLIRR) as the inner ring road in Kuala Lumpur, [Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 1](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Middle_Ring_Road_1 "Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 1") (KLMRR1), Kuala Lumpur Western/Northern Dispersal Link Scheme (Sprint Expressway and DUKE) and [Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 2](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Middle_Ring_Road_2 "Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 2") (KLMRR2) also act as middle ring roads of the city. [Kuala Lumpur–Kuala Selangor Expressway](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur%E2%80%93Kuala_Selangor_Expressway "Kuala Lumpur–Kuala Selangor Expressway") (KLS) (formerly [Assam Jawa–Templer Park Highway](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur%E2%80%93Kuala_Selangor_Expressway "Kuala Lumpur–Kuala Selangor Expressway") (LATAR)), [Kajang Dispersal Link Expressway](/wiki/Kajang_Dispersal_Link_Expressway "Kajang Dispersal Link Expressway") (SILK), [South Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/South_Klang_Valley_Expressway "South Klang Valley Expressway") (SKVE) and the planning [Kuala Lumpur Outer Ring Road](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Outer_Ring_Road "Kuala Lumpur Outer Ring Road") (KLORR) may form the outer ring roads of Kuala Lumpur.

Following the formation of the [Greater Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Greater_Kuala_Lumpur "Greater Kuala Lumpur") in the early 2010s, many expressways and highways will be built in the Greater Kuala Lumpur under the [Economic Transformation Programme](/wiki/Economic_Transformation_Programme "Economic Transformation Programme") (ETP). These are the [Besraya Extension Expressway](/wiki/Pandan_Indah_Link "Pandan Indah Link") (now part of the [Besraya Expressway](/wiki/Besraya_Expressway "Besraya Expressway")) (opened 2012), [Damansara–Shah Alam Elevated Expressway](/wiki/Damansara%E2%80%93Shah_Alam_Elevated_Expressway "Damansara–Shah Alam Elevated Expressway") (DASH), [Sungai Besi–Ulu Klang Elevated Expressway](/wiki/Sungai_Besi%E2%80%93Ulu_Klang_Elevated_Expressway "Sungai Besi–Ulu Klang Elevated Expressway") (SUKE), [East Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/East_Klang_Valley_Expressway "East Klang Valley Expressway") (EKVE) which will be part of the KLORR system, Sri Damansara Link and Tun Razak Link of the DUKE, [Kinrara–Damansara Expressway](/wiki/Kinrara%E2%80%93Damansara_Expressway "Kinrara–Damansara Expressway") (KIDEX Skyway) and [Serdang–Kinrara–Putrajaya Expressway](/wiki/Serdang%E2%80%93Kinrara%E2%80%93Putrajaya_Expressway "Serdang–Kinrara–Putrajaya Expressway") (SKIP). However, the proposed Kinrara–Damansara Expressway (KIDEX Skyway) project was officially scrapped by the Selangor State Government due to the several protests by the local [Petaling Jaya](/wiki/Petaling_Jaya "Petaling Jaya") citizens but it was rebranded as [Petaling Jaya Dispersal Link Expressway](/wiki/Petaling_Jaya_Dispersal_Link_Expressway "Petaling Jaya Dispersal Link Expressway") and also the [Bangi-Putrajaya Expressway](/wiki/Bangi-Putrajaya_Expressway "Bangi-Putrajaya Expressway").

### George Town Conurbation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: George Town Conurbation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/PenangBridge.jpg/330px-PenangBridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:PenangBridge.jpg)

The [Penang Bridge](/wiki/Penang_Bridge "Penang Bridge") across the [Penang Strait](/wiki/Penang_Strait "Penang Strait") was the first expressway in [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang"). The bridge was the longest bridge in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") upon its completion in 1985.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Cmglee_Penang_Second_Bridge_aerial.jpg/500px-Cmglee_Penang_Second_Bridge_aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cmglee_Penang_Second_Bridge_aerial.jpg)

The [Second Penang Bridge](/wiki/Sultan_Abdul_Halim_Muadzam_Shah_Bridge "Sultan Abdul Halim Muadzam Shah Bridge") lies to the south of the [Penang Bridge](/wiki/Penang_Bridge "Penang Bridge"). With a total length of 24 kilometres (15 mi), it is currently the second longest bridge in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").

The history of highways in [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang") began in the 1970s when the [Malaysian federal government](/wiki/Government_of_Malaysia "Government of Malaysia") decided to build the [Penang Bridge](/wiki/Penang_Bridge "Penang Bridge"), connecting [Seberang Perai](/wiki/Seberang_Perai "Seberang Perai") and [Penang Island](/wiki/Penang_Island "Penang Island"). The construction of the Penang Bridge, between [Perai](/wiki/Perai "Perai") on the [mainland](/wiki/Seberang_Perai "Seberang Perai") and [Gelugor](/wiki/Gelugor "Gelugor") on [Penang Island](/wiki/Penang_Island "Penang Island"), began in 1982 and was completed in 1985. This bridge was officially opened on 14 September 1985 by the fourth [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia"), [Mahathir Mohamad](/wiki/Mahathir_Mohamad "Mahathir Mohamad").

The main reasons for constructing new expressways in [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang") are the increasing populations in [George Town](/wiki/George_Town,_Penang "George Town, Penang") and [Butterworth](/wiki/Butterworth,_Penang "Butterworth, Penang"), and the need for more cross-strait linkages. Prior to the construction of the [Penang Bridge](/wiki/Penang_Bridge "Penang Bridge"), the only way to cross the [Penang Strait](/wiki/Penang_Strait "Penang Strait") between [Penang Island](/wiki/Penang_Island "Penang Island") and the mainland was via [ferries](/wiki/Ferry "Ferry"). In addition, major industrial estates, such as in [Bayan Lepas](/wiki/Bayan_Lepas "Bayan Lepas") on the [island](/wiki/Penang_Island "Penang Island") and [Perai](/wiki/Perai "Perai") on the [mainland](/wiki/Seberang_Perai "Seberang Perai"), were opened, leading to the growth of residential townships like [Bayan Baru](/wiki/Bayan_Baru "Bayan Baru") and [Seberang Jaya](/wiki/Seberang_Jaya "Seberang Jaya").[[15]](#cite_note-15) These necessitated the construction of more bridges and expressways in the state.

Since then, a number of other expressway projects within Penang, such as the [Butterworth Outer Ring Road](/wiki/Butterworth_Outer_Ring_Road "Butterworth Outer Ring Road") (BORR), the [Butterworth–Seberang Jaya Toll Road](/wiki/Butterworth%E2%80%93Seberang_Jaya_Toll_Road "Butterworth–Seberang Jaya Toll Road") and the [Tun Dr Lim Chong Eu Expressway](/wiki/Tun_Dr_Lim_Chong_Eu_Expressway "Tun Dr Lim Chong Eu Expressway") were completed. On densely populated [Penang Island](/wiki/Penang_Island "Penang Island"), the [Gelugor Highway](/wiki/Gelugor_Highway "Gelugor Highway") and the Penang Middle Ring Road were also created to alleviate traffic congestion.

The.[Butterworth–Kulim Expressway](/wiki/Butterworth%E2%80%93Kulim_Expressway "Butterworth–Kulim Expressway") (BKE) is a tolled expressway that connects [Butterworth](/wiki/Butterworth,_Penang "Butterworth, Penang") with the town of [Kulim](/wiki/Kulim_District "Kulim District") (and [Kulim Hi-tech Park](/wiki/Kulim_Hi-tech_Park "Kulim Hi-tech Park")) in neighbouring [Kedah](/wiki/Kedah "Kedah"). This interstate highway was built as industrialisation began to spread out from [Seberang Perai](/wiki/Seberang_Perai "Seberang Perai") towards southern [Kedah](/wiki/Kedah "Kedah") in the 1980s, forming what is now the [George Town Conurbation](/wiki/George_Town_Conurbation "George Town Conurbation").

The Second Penang Bridge, officially named the [Sultan Abdul Halim Muadzam Shah Bridge](/wiki/Sultan_Abdul_Halim_Muadzam_Shah_Bridge "Sultan Abdul Halim Muadzam Shah Bridge"), was opened on 1 March 2014 by the [Malaysian Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia"), [Najib Tun Razak](/wiki/Najib_Tun_Razak "Najib Tun Razak"). This 24-kilometre-long (15 mi) bridge, linking [Batu Maung](/wiki/Batu_Maung "Batu Maung") on [Penang Island](/wiki/Penang_Island "Penang Island") and [Batu Kawan](/wiki/Batu_Kawan "Batu Kawan") in [Seberang Perai](/wiki/Seberang_Perai "Seberang Perai"), is currently the longest bridge in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").

### Iskandar Malaysia and Johor Bahru

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Iskandar Malaysia and Johor Bahru")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Singapore-Johor_Causeway.jpg/330px-Singapore-Johor_Causeway.jpg)](/wiki/File:Singapore-Johor_Causeway.jpg)

The [Johor–Singapore Causeway](/wiki/Johor%E2%80%93Singapore_Causeway "Johor–Singapore Causeway") serves as the primary connection of Johor Bahru with Woodlands, [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"). Johor Bahru, the capital city of Johor, is visible in the background

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Malaysia-Singapore_Second_Link_Linkedua.jpg/330px-Malaysia-Singapore_Second_Link_Linkedua.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malaysia-Singapore_Second_Link_Linkedua.jpg)

The [Malaysia–Singapore Second Crossing](/wiki/Malaysia%E2%80%93Singapore_Second_Link "Malaysia–Singapore Second Link") serves as the secondary connection of Tanjung Kupang, Johor with Tuas, [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")

The history of highways in Johor Bahru started in the 1980s when the city of [Johor Bahru](/wiki/Johor_Bahru "Johor Bahru") became a main southern international gateway to Malaysia from [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") after the separation of Singapore from Malaysia on 9 August 1965.

The main reasons for building expressways in Johor Bahru are the increasing size of the Johor Bahru metropolitan area since it achieved city status on 1 January 1994, and the formation of the [South Johor Economic Region](/wiki/South_Johor_Economic_Region "South Johor Economic Region") (SJER) or Iskandar Development Region (IDR) (now [Iskandar Malaysia](/wiki/Iskandar_Malaysia "Iskandar Malaysia")) on 30 July 2006. Many townships have been constructed around Johor Bahru and industrial estates have been developed in areas such as [Senai](/wiki/Senai "Senai"), [Skudai](/wiki/Skudai "Skudai"), [Tebrau](/wiki/Tebrau "Tebrau"), [Pasir Gudang](/wiki/Pasir_Gudang "Pasir Gudang") and [Tampoi](/wiki/Tampoi,_Johor "Tampoi, Johor").

The first highway in Johor Bahru was [Skudai Highway](/wiki/Skudai_Highway "Skudai Highway") linking Senai to [Johor Causeway](/wiki/Johor_Causeway "Johor Causeway"), which was completed in 1985 and it was the first toll highway in Johor Bahru. However the toll plaza near Senai was abolished in 2004. [Kempas Highway](/wiki/Kempas_Highway "Kempas Highway"), the only state road in Malaysia constructed as a [2-lane highway](/wiki/Two-lane_expressway "Two-lane expressway") was completed in 1994. The [Malaysia–Singapore Second Crossing](/wiki/Malaysia%E2%80%93Singapore_Second_Link "Malaysia–Singapore Second Link"), which is the second link to Singapore after Johor Causeway, was opened to traffic on 18 April 1998.

Other expressway projects in Johor Bahru are [Senai–Desaru Expressway](/wiki/Senai%E2%80%93Desaru_Expressway "Senai–Desaru Expressway") (SDE) linking Senai in the west to Desaru in east coast of Johor, the [Johor Bahru Eastern Dispersal Link Expressway](/wiki/Johor_Bahru_Eastern_Dispersal_Link_Expressway "Johor Bahru Eastern Dispersal Link Expressway") (EDL) which linking Pandan interchange of the North–South Expressway to the new [Sultan Iskandar CIQ Building](/wiki/Sultan_Iskandar_Building "Sultan Iskandar Building") in city centre, the [Iskandar Coastal Highway](/wiki/Iskandar_Coastal_Highway "Iskandar Coastal Highway") linking [Nusajaya](/wiki/Nusajaya "Nusajaya") in the west to the city centre in the east and the [Johor Bahru East Coast Highway](/wiki/Johor_Bahru_East_Coast_Highway "Johor Bahru East Coast Highway") linking Kampung Bakar Batu passing through [Permas Jaya](/wiki/Permas_Jaya "Permas Jaya"), [Taman Rinting](/w/index.php?title=Taman_Rinting&action=edit&redlink=1 "Taman Rinting (page does not exist)") and finally towards Pasir Gudang.

In addition to [Johor Bahru Inner Ring Road](/wiki/Johor_Bahru_Inner_Ring_Road "Johor Bahru Inner Ring Road") (JBIRR) as the inner ring road in Johor Bahru, [Pasir Gudang Highway](/wiki/Pasir_Gudang_Highway "Pasir Gudang Highway"), [Second Link Expressway](/wiki/Second_Link_Expressway "Second Link Expressway") and [Johor Bahru Parkway](/wiki/Second_Link_Expressway "Second Link Expressway") also act as middle ring roads of the city. [Second Link Expressway](/wiki/Second_Link_Expressway "Second Link Expressway") and the [Senai–Desaru Expressway](/wiki/Senai%E2%80%93Desaru_Expressway "Senai–Desaru Expressway") may form the outer ring roads of Johor Bahru.

### East Malaysia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: East Malaysia")]

Main article: [Pan Borneo Highway](/wiki/Pan_Borneo_Highway "Pan Borneo Highway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/E24_Pending_Bridge.jpg/330px-E24_Pending_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:E24_Pending_Bridge.jpg)

[Tun Salahuddin Bridge](/wiki/Tun_Salahuddin_Bridge "Tun Salahuddin Bridge"), the only tolled expressway in East Malaysia. In the background is Kuching City Centre

The history of highways in East Malaysia started in the 1960s when the federal government decided to build the [Pan Borneo Highway](/wiki/Pan_Borneo_Highway "Pan Borneo Highway"), linking [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") and [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") state.

The Pan Borneo Highway project is a joint project between the governments of Brunei and Malaysia. The project started as soon as Sarawak and Sabah joined the federation of Malaysia in 1963. The lack of a road network system in Sarawak was the main factor of the construction.

There are one toll expressway, one toll federal highway and one toll state highway in [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") – the [Tun Salahuddin Bridge](/wiki/Tun_Salahuddin_Bridge "Tun Salahuddin Bridge") in [Kuching](/wiki/Kuching "Kuching") city, the [Miri–Baram Highway](/wiki/Miri%E2%80%93Baram_Highway "Miri–Baram Highway") in [Miri Division](/wiki/Miri_Division "Miri Division"), and the [Lanang Bridge](/wiki/Lanang_Bridge "Lanang Bridge") in [Sibu](/wiki/Sibu "Sibu"). The Tun Salahuddin Bridge is the first only toll expressway in East Malaysia. However, the toll collection of both Lanang Bridge and Tun Salahuddin Bridge were abolished in 2015 and 2016 by the Sarawak state government.

On 31 March 2015, the dual carriageway toll free Pan Borneo Expressway in [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") was unveiled by the Federal Government. The highway project will be implemented with [Lebuhraya Borneo Utara Sdn Bhd](/w/index.php?title=Lebuhraya_Borneo_Utara_Sdn_Bhd&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lebuhraya Borneo Utara Sdn Bhd (page does not exist)") (LBU) as the [Project Delivery Partner](/w/index.php?title=Project_Delivery_Partner&action=edit&redlink=1 "Project Delivery Partner (page does not exist)") (PDP) managing and supervising its construction.

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Features")]

### Expressway standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Expressway standards")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/BukitTinggiExit.PNG/250px-BukitTinggiExit.PNG)](/wiki/File:BukitTinggiExit.PNG)

Malaysian expressway exit signs

The construction, standards, management and usage of expressways in Malaysia are subject to Federal Roads Act (Private Management) 1984. In Malaysia, [expressways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") are defined as high-speed routes with at least four lanes (two in each direction) and should have [full access control](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") or partial access control. Most expressways in Malaysia are controlled-access expressways.

*Expressways* are defined as high-speed highways built under the JKR R6 rural highway standard, as [dual-carriageways](/wiki/Dual-carriageway "Dual-carriageway") of at least 4 lanes (2 lanes per carriageway) with full access control, grade-separated interchanges and high design speed limit of 120 kilometres per hour (75 mph), allowing the maximum speed limit of 110 kilometres per hour (68 mph).[[16]](#cite_note-arahan-teknik-16) However, the section between Cahaya Baru and Penawar of the [Senai–Desaru Expressway](/wiki/Senai%E2%80%93Desaru_Expressway "Senai–Desaru Expressway") E22 is built as a [two-lane](/wiki/Two-lane_expressway "Two-lane expressway") single carriageway with the similar features as the Swiss *[autostrasse](/wiki/Autostrasse "Autostrasse")*, making it as the first true two-lane controlled-access expressway in Malaysia followed by the section of Teluk Panglima Garang and Pulau Indah of the [South Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/South_Klang_Valley_Expressway "South Klang Valley Expressway") (SKVE) E26[[17]](#cite_note-blogjalanraya-2lane-17) All expressways are considered *federal highways*, but administered by [Malaysian Highway Authority](/wiki/Malaysian_Highway_Authority "Malaysian Highway Authority") (MHA) and the respective concessionaire companies.

*Highways*, on the other hand, complement the national network of expressways and federal roads and built under the JKR R5 rural highway standard, with relatively high design speed limit (although not as high as the expressway speed limit) of 100 kilometres per hour (62 mph), allowing the maximum speed limit of 90 kilometres per hour (56 mph).[[16]](#cite_note-arahan-teknik-16) The highways are built with partial access control, and grade-separated interchanges and at-grade crossings are both permitted. However, it is possible for a federal or state highway to be built with almost equivalent standard of an expressway with the exception of lower speed limit, for example the Federal Highway. Highways can be built either as dual-carriageway or two-lane [single carriageway](/wiki/Single_carriageway "Single carriageway").

Before the mid-1990s, there were no specific coding system for the expressways. When more and more expressways were built, a system of expressway numbering was applied to all expressways. Expressways are labelled with the letter "E" followed by assigned numbers, for example the code for North–South Expressway southern route is E2. The expressways have green signs and the text colour is white.

However, there are some exceptions in some highways. Some highways like Federal Highway (Federal Route 2) and [Skudai Highway](/wiki/Skudai_Highway "Skudai Highway") (Federal Route 1) retain their federal route codes. In addition, there are some highways in Malaysia which are classified as municipal roads such as [Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 1](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Middle_Ring_Road_1 "Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 1").

The highway exit numbers use the format "xxnn", where "xx" is the expressway code number (which can be one or two digits) and "nn" is the two-digit assigned number for each highway exit. For example, [Johor Bahru](/wiki/Johor_Bahru "Johor Bahru") exit at the end of [North–South Expressway](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_(Malaysia) "North–South Expressway (Malaysia)") is labelled exit 257, where the last two digits (57) are the assigned exit number and the first digit (2) is the expressway route number (E2). Meanwhile, [Jalan Templer](/wiki/Jalan_Templer "Jalan Templer") exit at the Federal Highway is labelled exit 224, where the two digits (24) are the assigned exit number and the first digit (2) is the federal route number (2). Expressways have distance markers in green colour (blue for [federal expressways and highways](/wiki/Malaysian_Federal_Roads_system "Malaysian Federal Roads system")) placed every 100 metres (330 ft; 110 yd).

### Pavements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Pavements")]

Most expressways are paved with typical [tarmac](/wiki/Tarmacadam "Tarmacadam"), which is a mixture of fine stone chips and tar; however, some expressways are paved with [concrete](/wiki/Concrete "Concrete") such as [North–South Expressway Northern Route](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_Northern_Route "North–South Expressway Northern Route") (from [Bukit Lanjan](/wiki/Bukit_Lanjan "Bukit Lanjan") Interchange, [Selangor](/wiki/Selangor "Selangor") to [Tapah](/wiki/Tapah "Tapah") interchange, [Perak](/wiki/Perak "Perak")), [New Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/New_Klang_Valley_Expressway "New Klang Valley Expressway"), [North–South Expressway Southern Route](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_Southern_Route "North–South Expressway Southern Route") (from [Ayer Keroh](/wiki/Ayer_Keroh "Ayer Keroh") interchange, [Melaka](/wiki/Melaka "Melaka") to [Tangkak](/wiki/Tangkak "Tangkak") interchange, [Johor](/wiki/Johor "Johor")), [SMART Tunnel](/wiki/SMART_Tunnel "SMART Tunnel") and [Skudai–Pontian Highway](/wiki/Skudai%E2%80%93Pontian_Highway "Skudai–Pontian Highway") (from [Universiti Teknologi Malaysia](/wiki/Universiti_Teknologi_Malaysia "Universiti Teknologi Malaysia") interchange to Taman Sri Pulai junction). Meanwhile, at Federal Highway linking Klang to Kuala Lumpur, the section of the expressway from [Subang Jaya](/wiki/Subang_Jaya "Subang Jaya") to [Kota Darul Ehsan](/wiki/Kota_Darul_Ehsan "Kota Darul Ehsan") near [Petaling Jaya](/wiki/Petaling_Jaya "Petaling Jaya") is paved with [asphalt](/wiki/Asphalt_concrete "Asphalt concrete").

## Expressway monitoring and maintenance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Expressway monitoring and maintenance")]

### Monitoring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Monitoring")]

Since 1986, Malaysian expressways have been built by private companies under the supervision of the government highway authority, Lembaga Lebuhraya Malaysia ([Malaysian Highway Authority](/wiki/Malaysian_Highway_Authority "Malaysian Highway Authority")). Every private concession company, such as [PLUS Expressways](/wiki/PLUS_Expressways "PLUS Expressways"), [ANIH Berhad](/wiki/ANIH_Berhad "ANIH Berhad") *(formerly MTD Prime)* and the others have monitored and maintained their expressways.

### Maintenance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Maintenance")]

See also: [PROPEL Berhad](/wiki/PROPEL_Berhad "PROPEL Berhad")

[Projek Penyelenggaraan Lebuhraya Berhad](/wiki/Projek_Penyelenggaraan_Lebuhraya_Berhad "Projek Penyelenggaraan Lebuhraya Berhad") (PROPEL) has undertaken repair and maintenance works on highway facilities, such as road works and repair works, road line painting, cleaning works on laybys and rest and service areas, trimming grass and landscaping along expressway areas, installing road furniture, and others. Meanwhile, the PROPEL Response Team Unit is a special response team unit.

The [Karak Expressway](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur%E2%80%93Karak_Expressway "Kuala Lumpur–Karak Expressway") and [East Coast Expressway](/wiki/East_Coast_Expressway "East Coast Expressway") are maintained by [Alloy Consolidated Sdn Bhd](/w/index.php?title=Alloy_Consolidated_Sdn_Bhd&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alloy Consolidated Sdn Bhd (page does not exist)").

### Traffic management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Traffic management")]

Since late 2006, every expressway in Malaysia has been monitored by the [Malaysian Highway Authority](/wiki/Malaysian_Highway_Authority "Malaysian Highway Authority") (LLM) Traffic Management Centre (LLM TMC). However, in some parts of [Klang Valley](/wiki/Klang_Valley "Klang Valley"), the expressways are monitored by the [Integrated Transport Information System](/wiki/Integrated_Transport_Information_System "Integrated Transport Information System") (ITIS); expressways in [George Town, Penang](/wiki/George_Town,_Penang "George Town, Penang") are monitored by the [Penang Island City Council](/wiki/Penang_Island_City_Council "Penang Island City Council").

## Toll system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Toll system")]

### Types of toll systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Types of toll systems")]

Every expressway and highway in Malaysia has a toll system, which is either a closed toll system or open toll system. All transactions are in [Malaysian Ringgit](/wiki/Malaysian_Ringgit "Malaysian Ringgit") (RM)

#### Open system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Open system")]

Users only have to pay at certain [toll plazas](/wiki/Toll_house "Toll house") within the open system range for a fixed amount.

#### Closed system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Closed system")]

Users collect toll tickets or touch in with their [Touch 'n Go](/wiki/Touch_%27n_Go "Touch 'n Go") card [North South Expressway](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_(Malaysia) "North–South Expressway (Malaysia)") issues the PLUSTransit cards and other closed toll expressways such as [East Coast Expressway](/wiki/East_Coast_Expressway "East Coast Expressway") and [South Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/South_Klang_Valley_Expressway "South Klang Valley Expressway") issues the transit card) before entering the expressway at respective toll plazas and pay an amount of toll or touch out with your same touch n go card at the exit toll plaza plus the distance from the plaza to the Limit of Maintenance Responsibility (LMR). The toll rate in this system is based on the distance traveled.

### Electronic toll collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Electronic toll collection")]

There are three types of the [electronic toll collection](/wiki/Electronic_toll_collection "Electronic toll collection") (ETC) systems, [Touch 'n Go](/wiki/Touch_%27n_Go "Touch 'n Go") card unit, [Smart TAG](/wiki/Smart_TAG "Smart TAG") on board unit, MyRFID tag and [contactless](/wiki/Contactless_payment "Contactless payment") credit/debit cards. Touch 'n Go and Smart TAG, have been made compulsory in all expressways since 1 July 2004, following the instruction of the Works Minister, [Datuk Seri S. Samy Vellu](/wiki/Samy_Vellu "Samy Vellu"). Other electronic payment systems that were previously used by other highway operators such as PLUS TAG for all [PLUS expressways network](/wiki/PLUS_Expressways_Berhad "PLUS Expressways Berhad"), Express TAG for [Shah Alam Expressway](/wiki/Shah_Alam_Expressway "Shah Alam Expressway"), FasTrak for [Damansara–Puchong Expressway](/wiki/Damansara%E2%80%93Puchong_Expressway "Damansara–Puchong Expressway") and [Sprint Expressway](/wiki/Sprint_Expressway "Sprint Expressway") and SagaTag in [Cheras–Kajang Expressway](/wiki/Cheras%E2%80%93Kajang_Expressway "Cheras–Kajang Expressway"), were abolished in a move to standardise the electronic payment method. Toll payments by contactless payment debit and credit cards using the MyDebit, [Visa](/wiki/Visa_Inc. "Visa Inc.") and [Mastercard](/wiki/Mastercard "Mastercard") network debuted in [Maju Expressway](/wiki/Maju_Expressway "Maju Expressway") since 3 August 2023.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Multi Lane Free Flow

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Multi Lane Free Flow")]

The [Multi Lane Free Flow](/wiki/Multi_Lane_Free_Flow "Multi Lane Free Flow") (MLFF) is an electronic toll collection system that allows free flow high speed toll system highway for all its users. With MLFF, the current toll lanes at toll plazas will be replaced with readers at gantry across the highway to detect vehicle and deduct toll using the existing ETC when fully implemented. The [Malaysian Highway Authority](/wiki/Malaysian_Highway_Authority "Malaysian Highway Authority") (MHA) is planning to implement MLFF system at all highways in stages starting 2010.

### Toll rebate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Toll rebate")]

Beginning 1 September 2009, the 20 per cent rebate given to motorists who pay toll charges more than 80 times a month, can be saved for up to six months. The rebate can be redeemed at 126 locations which would be announced in due time.

## Controversial issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Expressway_System&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Controversial issues")]

There are several controversial issues regarding the construction of expressways. The main issue is the increase of toll rates, which can be a huge burden especially for residents of [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur") and the surrounding Klang Valley conurbation.[[19]](#cite_note-blogjalanraya-tollprotest-19)

There are also various parties who question the capability of the numerous expressways in Klang Valley to overcome traffic congestion, which does not show signs of improvement with the construction of new expressways. Three chief factors were blamed for the urban expressway congestion, namely the short-sighted policies by the authorities, greedy property developers, and the failure of local municipal councils to control the development in the Klang Valley.[[20]](#cite_note-the-heat-20)

There are also several protests being held by residents of some housing areas being affected by several planned expressways, such as the [Sungai Besi–Ulu Klang Elevated Expressway](/wiki/Sungai_Besi%E2%80%93Ulu_Klang_Elevated_Expressway "Sungai Besi–Ulu Klang Elevated Expressway") (SUKE), [Damansara–Shah Alam Elevated Expressway](/wiki/Damansara%E2%80%93Shah_Alam_Elevated_Expressway "Damansara–Shah Alam Elevated Expressway") (DASH) and [Kinrara–Damansara Expressway](/wiki/Kinrara%E2%80%93Damansara_Expressway "Kinrara–Damansara Expressway") (KIDEX Skyway) (project scrapped in 2014). Environmental issues such as road noise and worsening congestion became the chief reasons of those protests.[[19]](#cite_note-blogjalanraya-tollprotest-19)

Other controversial issues include the following:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

1. The cracks found on beams on the Kepong Flyover in [Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 2](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Middle_Ring_Road_2 "Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 2") (MRR2) on 10 August 2004.
2. The flyover which collapsed on [Setia Alam Interchange](/w/index.php?title=Setia_Alam_Interchange&action=edit&redlink=1 "Setia Alam Interchange (page does not exist)") in [New Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/New_Klang_Valley_Expressway "New Klang Valley Expressway") during construction on 10 July 2005, where about 4 people died.
3. The 8 fallen I-beams at the [Pajam Interchange](/w/index.php?title=Pajam_Interchange&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pajam Interchange (page does not exist)") in [Negeri Sembilan](/wiki/Negeri_Sembilan "Negeri Sembilan") during the construction of the [Kajang–Seremban Highway](/wiki/Kajang%E2%80%93Seremban_Highway "Kajang–Seremban Highway") (LEKAS Highway) on the night of 27 September 2007.
4. The ramp collapse at the Batu Maung Interchange at [Batu Maung](/wiki/Batu_Maung "Batu Maung") side of the [Penang Second Bridge](/wiki/Sultan_Abdul_Halim_Muadzam_Shah_Bridge "Sultan Abdul Halim Muadzam Shah Bridge") during construction on 6 June 2013 killing one person.
